phase_metadata.description:
Master the art of performing multiple tasks
simultaneously in Java using Threads, Synchronization, and the modern Executor
Framework.

learning_objectives:
Understand
the difference between a Process and a Thread

Create threads using the Thread
class and Runnable interface
Manage the Thread Lifecycle and states
Prevent data corruption using the synchronized keyword

Implement
inter-thread communication using wait() and notify()

Utilize the Executor
Framework for professional thread management

Understand the basics of
asynchronous programming with CompletableFuture
introduction:
Multithreading is a specialized form of multitasking that
allows a single program to execute multiple parts (threads) concurrently. In
modern software, this is essential for keeping user interfaces responsive while
performing heavy calculations in the background, handling multiple client
requests in a server, or utilizing multi-core processors to speed up data
processing.

concept_explanation.what_is_it:
A thread is the smallest
unit of execution within a process. Multithreading allows a program to split
into two or more simultaneously running tasks.
concept_explanation.why_do_we_need_it:
1.
Improved Performance: Utilize multiple CPU cores. 2. Responsiveness: Background
tasks don't freeze the UI. 3. Efficiency: Threads share the same memory space,
making them 'lightweight' compared to processes.
concept_explanation.thread_vs_runnable:
The
Thread class is an object-oriented representation of a thread. The Runnable
interface represents the task to be performed. In modern Java, implementing
Runnable is preferred as it allows the class to extend another class (Java
doesn't support multiple inheritance).
concept_explanation.thread_lifecycle:
1. New: Created
but not started. 2. Runnable: Ready to run (waiting for CPU). 3. Running:
Actively executing. 4. Blocked/Waiting: Waiting for a resource or another
thread. 5. Terminated: Execution finished.
concept_explanation.synchronization:
When multiple
threads access shared data (like a bank balance), 'Race Conditions' can occur
where data becomes inconsistent. The 'synchronized' keyword ensures only one
thread can access a code block at a time.
concept_explanation.executor_framework:
Creating
threads manually with 'new Thread()' is expensive. The ExecutorService (Thread
Pool) manages a pool of worker threads, reusing them for different tasks to save
resources.

syntax.wait_notify:
lock.wait(); // Release lock and wait
lock.notify(); // Wake up
a waiting thread

syntax_breakdown.start_method:
Allocates memory for
a new thread and calls the run() method. Never call run() directly; that
executes in the current thread.
syntax_breakdown.join_method:
Causes the current thread to
wait until the thread it was called on completes.
syntax_breakdown.volatile_keyword:
Ensures
that changes to a variable are immediately visible to all threads by
reading/writing directly from main memory.
syntax_breakdown.synchronized_keyword:
Acquires
an 'intrinsic lock' or 'monitor' on an object, preventing other threads from
entering synchronized blocks of the same object.
syntax_breakdown.future_object:
A
placeholder for a result that will be available in the future after a task
completes.

rules_and_key_points:
A thread cannot be restarted once it
has finished execution.
The 'main' method runs on the main thread.
Synchronization only works if all threads lock on the same object.

Deadlock
occurs when two threads wait forever for locks held by each other.
Thread.sleep() does not release the lock it currently holds.

wait(),
notify(), and notifyAll() must be called inside a synchronized context.

Atomic classes (like AtomicInteger) provide thread-safe operations without
explicit synchronization.

Virtual Threads (Java 19+) are lightweight threads
intended to reduce the cost of blocking operations.

Always shut down an
ExecutorService when it is no longer needed.

Multithreading does not
guarantee the order of execution.

code_example_1.title:
Creating
Threads (Runnable vs Thread)
code_example_1.description:
The two standard ways to create
and start threads in Java.

description:
Using an ExecutorService
to handle concurrent updates to a shared counter.
output:
Thread class running...
Runnable Task: 1
Runnable Task: 2
Runnable
Task: 3
Final Count: 1000
step_by_step_dry_run:
1. In 'ConcurrencyDemo', a
Counter object is created on the heap with count = 0.
2. An ExecutorService
with a pool of 5 threads is initialized.
3. A loop submits 1000 increment tasks
to the pool.
4. When a thread picks a task, it tries to enter the 'increment'
method.
5. Because 'increment' is synchronized, the thread must acquire the
'lock' on the counter object.
6. If thread A holds the lock, thread B must wait
in the 'Blocked' state.
7. Once thread A finishes incrementing, it releases the
lock, and thread B can proceed.
8. This prevents two threads from reading 0 and
both writing 1 at the same time (Race Condition).
9. executor.shutdown() stops
accepting new tasks, and awaitTermination waits for the 1000 increments to
complete.
10. The final value 1000 is printed.
memory_visualization:
Process Memory: Shared by all threads. Contains the Heap (where 'Counter'
object resides).
Thread Stack: Each thread gets its own Stack memory. Contains
local variables and method call frames. 
Lock/Monitor: Every object on the Heap
has a hidden 'Header' used for locking. The 'synchronized' keyword utilizes this
header to block other threads.
real_world_use_cases:
Web Servers: Each
incoming HTTP request is handled by a separate thread from a pool.

Video
Rendering: Dividing a video frame into segments and rendering them in
parallel.

Database Connection Pools: Reusing existing connections instead of
creating new ones.

Mobile Apps: Fetching data from an API on a background
thread so the UI doesn't stutter.
common_mistakes:
Calling run()
instead of start(): run() executes in the current thread like a normal method
call.

Shared data without synchronization: Leads to unpredictable results
(Heisenbugs).

Not shutting down ExecutorService: Can prevent the JVM from
exiting.

Using synchronization too much: Excessive locking leads to poor
performance and potential deadlocks.

Ignoring InterruptedException: This
exception is the signal to a thread that it should stop what it's doing.
best_practices:
Prefer ExecutorService over creating raw 'new Thread()'
objects.
Implement Runnable or Callable rather than extending Thread.

Keep
synchronized blocks as small as possible to minimize blocking.

Use
java.util.concurrent.atomic classes for simple counters.

Always name your
threads (especially in thread pools) to make debugging easier.

Use 'volatile'
only for visibility, not for atomicity.

faq.question:
What is the
difference between yield() and sleep()?
faq.answer:
sleep() pauses the thread
for a specified duration. yield() is a hint to the scheduler that the thread is
willing to give up its current use of the CPU, but it stays in the Runnable
state.

faq.question: Can a synchronized method be static?
faq.answer:
Yes.
A static synchronized method locks on the 'Class' object rather than the
instance 'this' object.

faq.question: What is a Daemon thread?
faq.answer:
A daemon thread is a service provider thread that doesn't prevent the JVM from
exiting when the program finishes. The Garbage Collector is a daemon thread.

summary:
Phase 13 explored Multithreading, the capability of Java to
execute concurrent tasks. We learned how to create threads, manage their
lifecycle, and solve synchronization issues to prevent data corruption. Finally,
we looked at the Executor Framework as the professional way to manage thread
pools in modern Java applications.
key_takeaways:
Threads share memory;
Processes do not.
Always use .start() to initiate a thread.

Runnable is
more flexible than extending Thread.

Synchronization prevents Race Conditions
on shared variables.
Wait/Notify are used for inter-thread communication.
ExecutorService is the standard way to manage thread pools.

Join() makes one
thread wait for another to finish.

Deadlock is a circular dependency on
locks.
The volatile keyword ensures variable visibility across threads.
Java 21 Virtual Threads are optimized for I/O heavy tasks.
preview_of_the_next_topic:
In Phase 14, we will learn about File I/O,
exploring how to read from and write to the computer's file system using Java.

questions.question:
What is
the main difference between a process and a thread?
questions.answer:
A process is an
independent execution environment with its own memory space (private heap). A
thread is a subset of a process that shares the memory space and resources of
the process it belongs to.

questions.question:
Why is implementing 'Runnable' generally better than extending
'Thread'?
questions.answer:
Implementing 'Runnable' is better because: 1. It supports
multiple inheritance (your class can still extend another class). 2. It promotes
better separation of concerns (task vs. execution mechanism). 3. It is
compatible with modern functional programming and lambdas.

questions.question:
How can you ensure that the
'main' thread finishes only after a secondary thread 't1' has completed its
work?
questions.answer:
You should call the 't1.join()' method. This will block the
'main' thread until 't1' finishes its execution.

questions.question:
In a Producer-Consumer scenario, why
must wait() and notify() be called inside a synchronized block?
questions.answer:
They must be called inside a synchronized block because they require the thread
to own the object's monitor (lock). Calling wait() releases the lock so other
threads can proceed, and notify() wakes up a thread that is waiting for that
same lock.

questions.question: Hi
Hard

Explain 'Deadlock' and how to
avoid it.
questions.answer:
Deadlock happens when Thread 1 holds Lock A and waits for
Lock B, while Thread 2 holds Lock B and waits for Lock A. To avoid it, always
acquire locks in the same consistent order across all threads, or use timed lock
attempts (tryLock) from the java.util.concurrent.locks package.

questions.question:
Write a snippet using
an ExecutorService that calculates the square of numbers from 1 to 5 using a
pool of 2 threads.

Implement a simple
thread-safe Singleton class using the 'Double-Checked Locking' pattern.

mcqs.question:
Which state
is a thread in after it is created but before start() is called?

Which interface should you implement to create a task that
returns a value?

What is the primary purpose of the
'volatile' keyword?

options.B:
To ensure
thread visibility of variables
options.D:
To
prevent a variable from being serialized

mcqs.question:
Which method is used to pause the current thread for a specific amount of
time?

options.A:
wait() must be inside a
loop
options.B: wait() throws a checked InterruptedException that must be handled
options.D:
The method should not be
synchronized

mcqs.question:
What happens when you call
t.join()?

options.A: The thread 't' joins the current thread pool
options.B: The current thread waits until 't' finishes
options.C:
The thread 't' waits
until the main thread finishes

mcqs.question:
Which method wakes up all threads waiting on an object's
monitor?

Best Approach: You need to
run 1000 independent tasks. Which approach is best?

options.B:
Use a single thread and run them one
by one
options.C: Use an ExecutorService with a fixed thread pool
options.D:
Use the
main thread for everything
